The **Sikh Empire**[[7]](#cite_note-8) was a [regional power](/wiki/Regional_power "Regional power") based in the [Punjab region](/wiki/Punjab "Punjab") of the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent").[[4]](#cite_note-Duggal-5)[[8]](#cite_note-9) It existed from 1799, when [Maharaja](/wiki/Maharaja "Maharaja") [Ranjit Singh](/wiki/Ranjit_Singh "Ranjit Singh") captured [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), to 1849, when it was defeated and conquered by the [British East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") following the [Second Anglo-Sikh War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Sikh_War "Second Anglo-Sikh War").[[1]](#cite_note-Encyclopædia_Britannica_Eleventh_Edition_1911_Page_892-1) At its peak in the mid-19th century the empire extended from [Gilgit](/wiki/Gilgit "Gilgit") and [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet_under_Qing_rule "Tibet under Qing rule") in the north to the [deserts of Sindh](/wiki/Thar_Desert "Thar Desert") in the south and from the [Khyber Pass](/wiki/Khyber_Pass "Khyber Pass") in the west to the [Sutlej](/wiki/Sutlej "Sutlej") in the east,[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGupta1991201-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11) and was divided into eight provinces.[[a]](#cite_note-12)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGupta1991334–336-13) Religiously diverse, with an estimated population of 4.5 million in 1831 (making it the [19th most populous state at the time](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_population_in_1800 "List of countries by population in 1800")),[[12]](#cite_note-:12-14) it was the last major region of the Indian subcontinent to be annexed by the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj").

In 1799, Ranjit Singh of [Sukerchakia Misl](/wiki/Sukerchakia_Misl "Sukerchakia Misl") captured Lahore from the Sikh [triumvirate](/wiki/Triumvirate "Triumvirate") which had been ruling it [since 1765](/wiki/Sikh_period_in_Lahore#Sikh_triumvirate "Sikh period in Lahore"), and was confirmed on the possession of Lahore by the Durrani ruler, [Zaman Shah](/wiki/Zaman_Shah_Durrani "Zaman Shah Durrani").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGupta199126–30-15) He was formally crowned on 12 April 1801 by [Sahib Singh Bedi](/wiki/Sahib_Singh_Bedi "Sahib Singh Bedi"), a descendant of [Guru Nanak](/wiki/Guru_Nanak "Guru Nanak").[[14]](#cite_note-16) Ranjit Singh rose to power in a very short period, from a leader of a single [misl](/wiki/Sikh_Confederacy "Sikh Confederacy") to finally becoming the Maharaja of Punjab. By 1813 all the remaining Sikh misls had been annexed by Ranjit Singh,[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGupta199137–55-17) and the following years saw [progressive expulsion](/wiki/Afghan-Sikh_Wars "Afghan-Sikh Wars") of the [Afghans](/wiki/Durrani_Empire "Durrani Empire") from Punjab; the Afghan influence east of [Indus](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") ended after the [fall of Multan](/wiki/Siege_of_Multan_(1818) "Siege of Multan (1818)") in 1818. In the subsequent decades Durrani Afghans lost [Kashmir](/wiki/Battle_of_Shopian "Battle of Shopian") and [Peshawar](/wiki/Capture_of_Peshawar_(1834) "Capture of Peshawar (1834)") to the Sikhs as well. By 1840 [Ladakh](/wiki/Ladakh "Ladakh") and [Baltistan](/wiki/Baltistan "Baltistan") had been brought under Sikh suzerainty by [Gulab Singh](/wiki/Gulab_Singh "Gulab Singh"). Ranjit Singh modernised his army using the latest training as well as weapons and [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery").

After the death of Maharaja Ranjit Singh in 1839, the empire was weakened by the British East India Company stoking internal divisions and political mismanagement. Finally, in 1849, the state was dissolved after its defeat in the [Second Anglo-Sikh War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Sikh_War "Second Anglo-Sikh War").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Terminology

The empire is also referred to as the *Lahore State*, such as in contemporary British maps.[[16]](#cite_note-18)[[17]](#cite_note-19)[[18]](#cite_note-:11-20) The term *Lahore Darbar* refers to the [Sikh court](/wiki/Durbar_(court) "Durbar (court)") at Lahore of the empire's ruling government.[[note 2]](#cite_note-21)[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) However, the term "Lahore Darbar" gained currency only around the time of Ranjit Singh's death, and was not found in British sources until then.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) The empire's own Persian chronicles refers to its ruling government as the *Sarkar Khalsaji*.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) According to Priya Atwal, the polity was known in Punjabi as *Sarkar-i-Khalsa*.[[20]](#cite_note-:15-23) The contemporary British and other Europeans referred to the state as the *Kingdom of Lahore* and its ruling family as the Lahore royal family.[[20]](#cite_note-:15-23) The term *Sikh Empire* came into popular usage in the 20th century, perhaps as an English-language equivalent to the Sarkar-i-Khalsa term.[[20]](#cite_note-:15-23)

## History

### Background

The foundation of the Sikh Empire can be traced to as early as 1707, the year of [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb")'s death and the start of the downfall of the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire"). With the Mughals significantly weakened, the Sikh army, known as the [Dal Khalsa](/wiki/Dal_Khalsa_(Sikh_Empire) "Dal Khalsa (Sikh Empire)"), a rearrangement of the [Khalsa Fauj](/wiki/Khalsa_Fauj "Khalsa Fauj") inaugurated by [Guru Gobind Singh](/wiki/Guru_Gobind_Singh "Guru Gobind Singh"), led expeditions against them and the [Afghans](/wiki/Durrani_Empire "Durrani Empire") in the west. This led to a growth of the army which split into different confederacies or semi-independent *misls*. Each of these component armies controlled different areas and cities. However, in the period from 1762 to 1799, Sikh commanders of the *misls* appeared to be coming into their own as independent.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Mughal rule of Punjab

[Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism") began during the conquest of [North India](/wiki/North_India "North India") by [Babur](/wiki/Babur "Babur"), the founder of the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire"). His grandson, [Akbar](/wiki/Akbar_the_Great "Akbar the Great"), supported religious freedom and after visiting the [langar](/wiki/Langar_(Sikhism) "Langar (Sikhism)") of [Guru Amar Das](/wiki/Guru_Amar_Das "Guru Amar Das") got a favourable impression of Sikhism. As a result of his visit, he donated land to the langar and the Mughals did not have any conflict with [Sikh gurus](/wiki/Sikh_gurus "Sikh gurus") until his death in 1605.[[21]](#cite_note-24)

His successor [Jahangir](/wiki/Jahangir "Jahangir"), saw the Sikhs as a political threat. He ordered [Guru Arjan](/wiki/Guru_Arjun_Dev "Guru Arjun Dev"), who had been arrested for supporting the rebellious [Khusrau Mirza](/wiki/Khusrau_Mirza "Khusrau Mirza"),[[22]](#cite_note-25) to change the passage about Islam[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] in the [Adi Granth](/wiki/Adi_Granth "Adi Granth"). When the Guru refused, Jahangir ordered him to be put to death by torture.[[23]](#cite_note-26) Guru Arjan's [martyrdom](/wiki/Martyrdom_in_Sikhism "Martyrdom in Sikhism") led to the sixth Guru, [Guru Hargobind](/wiki/Guru_Hargobind "Guru Hargobind"), declaring Sikh [sovereignty](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") in the creation of the [Akal Takht](/wiki/Akal_Takht "Akal Takht") and the establishment of a fort to defend [Amritsar](/wiki/Amritsar "Amritsar").[[24]](#cite_note-Jestice_2004_pages=345-346-27)

Jahangir attempted to assert authority over the Sikhs by jailing Guru Hargobind at [Gwalior Fort](/wiki/Gwalior_Fort "Gwalior Fort"), but released him after a number of years when he no longer felt threatened. The Sikh community did not have any further issues with the Mughal Empire until the death of Jahangir in 1627. The succeeding son of Jahangir, [Shah Jahan](/wiki/Shah_Jahan "Shah Jahan"), took offence at Guru Hargobind's "sovereignty" and after a series of assaults on Amritsar forced the Sikhs to retreat to the [Sivalik Hills](/wiki/Sivalik_Hills "Sivalik Hills").[[24]](#cite_note-Jestice_2004_pages=345-346-27)

The next guru, [Guru Har Rai](/wiki/Guru_Har_Rai "Guru Har Rai"), maintained the guruship in these hills by defeating local attempts to seize Sikh land and playing a neutral role in the power struggle between two of the sons of Shah Jahan, [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb") and [Dara Shikoh](/wiki/Dara_Shikoh "Dara Shikoh"), for control of the Mughal Empire. The ninth Guru, [Guru Tegh Bahadur](/wiki/Guru_Tegh_Bahadur "Guru Tegh Bahadur"), moved the Sikh community to [Anandpur](/wiki/Anandpur_Sahib "Anandpur Sahib") and travelled extensively to visit and preach in defiance of Aurangzeb, who attempted to install the [excommunicated](/wiki/Patit "Patit") [Ram Rai](/wiki/Ram_Rai "Ram Rai") as the new guru. Guru Tegh Bahadur aided [Kashmiri Pandits](/wiki/Kashmiri_Pandits "Kashmiri Pandits") in avoiding conversion to [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") and was arrested under the orders of Aurangzeb. When offered a choice between conversion to Islam and death, he chose to die rather than compromise his principles and was executed.[[25]](#cite_note-28)

##### Formation of the Khalsa

[Guru Gobind Singh](/wiki/Guru_Gobind_Singh "Guru Gobind Singh") assumed the guruship in 1675 and to avoid battles with Sivalik Hill rajas moved the guruship to [Paonta](/wiki/Paonta_Sahib "Paonta Sahib"). There he built a large fort to protect the city and garrisoned an army to protect it. The growing power of the Sikh community alarmed the [Shivalik Hill rajas](/wiki/Hill_States_of_India "Hill States of India"), who attempted to attack the city, but [Guru Gobind Singh](/wiki/Guru_Gobind_Singh "Guru Gobind Singh")'s forces routed them at the [Battle of Bhangani](/wiki/Battle_of_Bhangani "Battle of Bhangani"). He moved on to Anandpur and established the [Khalsa](/wiki/Khalsa "Khalsa"), a collective army of baptised Sikhs, on 30 March 1699.[[26]](#cite_note-Gobind-29)

The establishment of the Khalsa united the Sikh community against various Mughal-backed claimants to the guruship.[[27]](#cite_note-30) In 1701, a combined army of the Sivalik Hill rajas and the Mughals under [Wazir Khan](/wiki/Wazir_Khan_(Sirhind) "Wazir Khan (Sirhind)") attacked Anandpur. The Khalsa retreated but regrouped to defeat the Mughals at the [Battle of Muktsar](/wiki/Battle_of_Muktsar "Battle of Muktsar"). In 1707, [Guru Gobind Singh](/wiki/Guru_Gobind_Singh "Guru Gobind Singh") accepted an invitation by Aurangzeb's successor [Bahadur Shah I](/wiki/Bahadur_Shah_I "Bahadur Shah I") to meet him. The meeting took place at [Agra](/wiki/Agra "Agra") on 23 July 1707.[[26]](#cite_note-Gobind-29)

##### Banda Singh Bahadur

In August 1708, Guru Gobind Singh visited [Nanded](/wiki/Nanded "Nanded"). There he met a Bairāgī recluse, Madho Das, who converted to Sikhism, rechristened as [Banda Singh Bahadur](/wiki/Banda_Singh_Bahadur "Banda Singh Bahadur").[[26]](#cite_note-Gobind-29)[[28]](#cite_note-Banda_Singh_Bahadur-31) A short time before his death, Guru Gobind Singh ordered him to reconquer Punjab region and gave him a letter that commanded all Sikhs to join him. After two years of gaining supporters, Banda Singh Bahadur initiated an agrarian uprising by breaking up the large estates of [zamindar families](/wiki/Zamindar#Mughal_era "Zamindar") and distributing the land to the poor [peasants](/wiki/Ryot "Ryot") who farmed the land.[[29]](#cite_note-Singh_2008_pages=25-26-32)

Banda Singh Bahadur started his rebellion by defeating the Mughal armies at the [Battle of Samana](/wiki/Battle_of_Samana "Battle of Samana"), establishing the First Sikh State in 1709. This was followed the next year by another Sikh victory at the [Battle of Sadhaura](/wiki/Battle_of_Sadhaura "Battle of Sadhaura"). The rebellion culminated following their defeat at the [Siege of Gurdaspur](/wiki/Siege_of_Gurdaspur "Siege of Gurdaspur"). During the rebellion, Banda Singh Bahadur made a point of destroying the cities in which Mughals had been cruel to the supporters of Guru Gobind Singh. He executed Wazir Khan in revenge for the deaths of Guru Gobind Singh's sons and Pir Budhu Shah after the Sikh victory at [Sirhind](/wiki/Sirhind "Sirhind").[[30]](#cite_note-33)

He ruled the territory between the [Sutlej river](/wiki/Sutlej_river "Sutlej river") and the [Yamuna river](/wiki/Yamuna_river "Yamuna river"), established a capital in the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") at [Lohgarh](/wiki/Lohgarh_(Bilaspur) "Lohgarh (Bilaspur)") and struck [coinage](/wiki/Coin "Coin") in the names of [Guru Nanak](/wiki/Guru_Nanak "Guru Nanak") and Guru Gobind Singh.[[29]](#cite_note-Singh_2008_pages=25-26-32) In 1716, his army was defeated by the Mughals after he attempted to defend his fort at [Gurdas Nangal](/wiki/Battle_of_Gurdas_Nangal "Battle of Gurdas Nangal"). He was captured along with 700 of his men and sent to [Delhi](/wiki/Old_Delhi "Old Delhi"), where they were all tortured and executed after refusing to convert to [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam").[[31]](#cite_note-34)

#### Dal Khalsa period

Main article: [Dal Khalsa (Sikh Army)](/wiki/Dal_Khalsa_(Sikh_Army) "Dal Khalsa (Sikh Army)")

##### Sikh Confederacy

Main article: [Misl](/wiki/Misl "Misl")

The period from 1716 to 1799 was a highly turbulent time politically and militarily in the Punjab region. This was caused by the overall decline of the Mughal empire[[32]](#cite_note-35) that left a power vacuum in the region that was eventually filled by the Sikhs of the [Dal Khalsa](/wiki/Dal_Khalsa_(Sikh_Army) "Dal Khalsa (Sikh Army)"), meaning "[Khalsa](/wiki/Khalsa "Khalsa") army" or "[Khalsa](/wiki/Khalsa "Khalsa") party". In the late 18th century, after defeating several invasions by the Afghan rulers of the [Durrani Empire](/wiki/Durrani_Empire "Durrani Empire") and their allies,[[33]](#cite_note-The_History_of_Afghanistan-36) remnants of the Mughals and their [viceroys](/wiki/Nawabs "Nawabs"), the Mughal-allied Hindu hill rajas of the [Sivalik Hills](/wiki/Sivalik_Hills "Sivalik Hills"),[[34]](#cite_note-37)[[35]](#cite_note-38) and hostile local Muslims siding with other Muslim forces.[[33]](#cite_note-The_History_of_Afghanistan-36) The Sikhs of the [Dal Khalsa](/wiki/Dal_Khalsa_(Sikh_Army) "Dal Khalsa (Sikh Army)") eventually formed their own independent Sikh administrative regions, *Misls*, derived from a Perso-Arabic term meaning 'similar', headed by *Misldars*. These Misls were united in large part by Maharaja Ranjit Singh.

##### Intra-Misl Wars

After the reign of [Jassa Singh Ramgarhia](/wiki/Jassa_Singh_Ramgarhia "Jassa Singh Ramgarhia"), the Sikh Misls became divided and fought each other. A sort of 'Cold War' broke out with the [Bhangi](/wiki/Bhangi_Misl "Bhangi Misl"), [Nakkai](/wiki/Nakai_Misl "Nakai Misl"), [Dalelwala](/wiki/Dallewalia_Misl "Dallewalia Misl") and [Ramgharia](/wiki/Ramgarhia_Misl "Ramgarhia Misl") misls verses [Sukerchakia](/wiki/Sukerchakia_Misl "Sukerchakia Misl"), [Ahluwalia](/wiki/Ahluwalia_(misl) "Ahluwalia (misl)"), [Karor Singhia](/wiki/Karorsinghia_Misl "Karorsinghia Misl") and [Kaniyeha](/wiki/Kanhaiya_Misl "Kanhaiya Misl"). The [Shaheedan](/wiki/Shaheedan_Misl "Shaheedan Misl"), [Nishania](/wiki/Nishanwalia_Misl "Nishanwalia Misl") and [Singhpuria](/wiki/Singhpuria_Misl "Singhpuria Misl") also allied but did not engage in warfare with the others and continued the Dal Khalsa.

The [Phulkian Misl](/wiki/Phulkian_Misl "Phulkian Misl") was excommunicated from the confederacy. [Sada Kaur](/wiki/Sada_Kaur "Sada Kaur") of the Kanhaiya Misl rose in the vacuum and destroyed the power of the Bhangis. She later gave her throne to Maharaja Ranjit Singh.

### Empire

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/9c/Punjabin_1809_AD-History_of_Punjab_pg32.jpg/250px-Punjabin_1809_AD-History_of_Punjab_pg32.jpg)](/wiki/File:Punjabin_1809_AD-History_of_Punjab_pg32.jpg)

The expanding Sikh Empire in 1809. The [Cis-Sutlej states](/wiki/Cis-Sutlej_states "Cis-Sutlej states") are visible south of the [Sutlej River](/wiki/Sutlej "Sutlej")

The formal start of the Sikh Empire began with the unification of the Misls by 1801, creating a unified political state. All the Misl leaders, who were affiliated with the army, were the nobility with usually long and prestigious family backgrounds in Sikh history.[[1]](#cite_note-Encyclopædia_Britannica_Eleventh_Edition_1911_Page_892-1)

The main geographical footprint of the empire was from the [Punjab region](/wiki/Punjab_region "Punjab region") to [Khyber Pass](/wiki/Khyber_Pass "Khyber Pass") in the west, to Kashmir in the north, [Sindh](/wiki/Talpur_dynasty "Talpur dynasty") in the south, and [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet_under_Qing_rule "Tibet under Qing rule") in the east.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGupta1991201-10)

In 1797, [Ranjit Singh](/wiki/Ranjit_Singh "Ranjit Singh") besieged [Rasulnagar](/wiki/Rasool_Nagar "Rasool Nagar") and annexed the Chattha State who had been bitter enemies of [Sukerchakia Misl](/wiki/Sukerchakia_Misl "Sukerchakia Misl"), the ruler Jan Muhammad Chattha was killed in the siege.[[36]](#cite_note-39)

In 1799, [Ranjit Singh](/wiki/Ranjit_Singh "Ranjit Singh") moved the capital to [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore") from [Gujranwala](/wiki/Gujranwala "Gujranwala"), where it had been established in 1763 by his grandfather, [Charat Singh](/wiki/Charat_Singh "Charat Singh").[[37]](#cite_note-40)

Ranjit Singh annexed the [Sial State](/wiki/Sial_dynasty "Sial dynasty"), a local Muslim-ruled chieftaincy, after invading [Jhang](/wiki/Jhang "Jhang") in 1807.[[38]](#cite_note-:6-41) The basis for this annexation was that the local ruler of Jhang, Ahmad Khan Sial, was conspiring with [Nawab Muzaffar Khan](/wiki/Nawab_Muzaffar_Khan "Nawab Muzaffar Khan") of Multan and had signed a secret treaty with the latter.[[38]](#cite_note-:6-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Lithograph_titled_%27Private_Native_Durbar%27%2C_published_in_%27The_Court_and_Camp_of_Runjeet_Sing%27_%281840%29.png/250px-Lithograph_titled_%27Private_Native_Durbar%27%2C_published_in_%27The_Court_and_Camp_of_Runjeet_Sing%27_%281840%29.png)](/wiki/File:Lithograph_titled_%27Private_Native_Durbar%27,_published_in_%27The_Court_and_Camp_of_Runjeet_Sing%27_(1840).png)

[Ranjit Singh](/wiki/Ranjit_Singh "Ranjit Singh") holding court in 1838

[Hari Singh Nalwa](/wiki/Hari_Singh_Nalwa "Hari Singh Nalwa") was [Commander-in-Chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of the [Sikh Khalsa Army](/wiki/Sikh_Khalsa_Army "Sikh Khalsa Army") from 1825 to 1837.[[39]](#cite_note-google-42) He is known for his role in the conquests of [Kasur](/wiki/Kasur "Kasur"), [Sialkot](/wiki/Sialkot "Sialkot"), [Multan](/wiki/Multan "Multan"), [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir"), [Attock](/wiki/Attock "Attock") and [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"). Nalwa led the Sikh army in freeing [Shah Shuja](/wiki/Shuja_Shah_Durrani "Shuja Shah Durrani") from Kashmir and secured the [Koh-i-Nor](/wiki/Koh-i-Nor "Koh-i-Nor") diamond for Maharaja Ranjit Singh. He served as governor of Kashmir and [Hazara](/wiki/Hazara_region "Hazara region") and established a [mint](/wiki/Mint_(coin) "Mint (coin)") on behalf of the Sikh empire to facilitate revenue collection. His frontier policy of holding the [Khyber Pass](/wiki/Khyber_Pass "Khyber Pass") was later used by the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"). Nalwa was responsible for expanding the frontier of Sikh empire to the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River"). At the time of his death, the western boundary of the Sikh Empire was the Khyber Pass.

The [Namgyal dynasty of Ladakh](/wiki/Namgyal_dynasty_of_Ladakh "Namgyal dynasty of Ladakh") paid regular annual tribute to the Sikh Empire starting 1819 until 1834.[[40]](#cite_note-:1-43) The tribute was paid to the local Sikh governors of Kashmir.[[40]](#cite_note-:1-43) The Namgyal kingdom would [later be conquered](/wiki/Dogra_invasion_of_Ladakh "Dogra invasion of Ladakh") by the [Dogras](/wiki/Dogra_dynasty "Dogra dynasty"), under the leadership of Zorawar Singh.[[41]](#cite_note-:2-44)

The domain of the [Maqpon dynasty](/wiki/Maqpon_dynasty "Maqpon dynasty") of [Baltistan](/wiki/Baltistan "Baltistan"), based in [Skardu](/wiki/Skardu "Skardu"), under the rule of Ahmad Shah Maqpon, was [conquered in 1839–40](/wiki/Dogra_invasion_of_Baltistan "Dogra invasion of Baltistan") and its local ruler was deposed.[[41]](#cite_note-:2-44)[[42]](#cite_note-:3-45)[[43]](#cite_note-:4-46)[[44]](#cite_note-:5-47) The Dogras at this time were under the suzerainty of the Sikh Empire.[[41]](#cite_note-:2-44)

During the [Sino-Sikh War](/wiki/Sino-Sikh_war "Sino-Sikh war") of 1841, the forces of the empire invaded [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet_under_Qing_rule "Tibet under Qing rule"), which was then under the control of the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty").[[45]](#cite_note-:7-48) However, this control was short-lived and the military of the empire was forced to retreat to Ladakh due to a counterattack by the Chinese and Tibetans.[[45]](#cite_note-:7-48)

#### Cis-Sutlej states

Main article: [Cis-Sutlej states](/wiki/Cis-Sutlej_states "Cis-Sutlej states")

The [Cis-Sutlej states](/wiki/Cis-Sutlej_states "Cis-Sutlej states") were a group of Sikh[[46]](#cite_note-49) states in the Punjab region lying between the [Sutlej River](/wiki/Sutlej_River "Sutlej River") to the north, the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") to the east, the [Yamuna River](/wiki/Yamuna_River "Yamuna River") and [Delhi](/wiki/Old_Delhi "Old Delhi") district to the south, and [Sirsa District](/wiki/Sirsa_District "Sirsa District") to the west. These states fell under the [suzerainty](/wiki/Suzerainty "Suzerainty") of the [Maratha Empire](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire") after 1785 before the [Second Anglo-Maratha War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Maratha_War "Second Anglo-Maratha War") of 1803–1805, after which the Marathas lost control of the territory to the [British East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company"). The Cis-Sutlej states included [Kalsia](/wiki/Kalsia "Kalsia"), [Kaithal](/wiki/Kaithal_State "Kaithal State"), [Patiala](/wiki/Patiala_State "Patiala State"), [Nabha](/wiki/Nabha_State "Nabha State"), [Jind](/wiki/Jind_State "Jind State"), [Thanesar](/wiki/Thanesar "Thanesar"), [Malerkotla](/wiki/Maler_Kotla "Maler Kotla"), [Ludhiana](/wiki/Ludhiana "Ludhiana"), [Kapurthala](/wiki/Kapurthala_State "Kapurthala State"), [Ambala](/wiki/Ambala "Ambala"), [Ferozpur](/wiki/Ferozpur "Ferozpur") and [Faridkot](/wiki/Faridkot_State "Faridkot State"), among others.[[47]](#cite_note-50) The Sikh Empire of [Ranjit Singh](/wiki/Ranjit_Singh "Ranjit Singh") occupied Faridkot State in 1807.[[48]](#cite_note-:02-51) However, control was restored to Gulab Singh of Faridkot in 1809 due to the signing of a treaty between the Lahore Darbar and the British East India Company.[[48]](#cite_note-:02-51)

While these Sikh states had been set up by the [Dal Khalsa](/wiki/Dal_Khalsa_(Sikh_Army) "Dal Khalsa (Sikh Army)"), they did not become part of the Sikh Empire. There was a mutual ban on warfare following the [treaty of Amritsar](/wiki/Treaty_of_Amritsar_(1809) "Treaty of Amritsar (1809)") in 1809 (in which the empire forfeited the claim to the Cis-Sutlej States, and the British were not to interfere north of the Sutlej or in the empire's existing territory south of the Sutlej),[[49]](#cite_note-52) following attempts by Ranjit Singh to wrest control of these states from the British between 1806 and 1809[[50]](#cite_note-53)[[51]](#cite_note-54)

The Sikh crossing of the Sutlej, following British militarisation of the border with Punjab (from 2,500 men and six guns in 1838 to 17,612 men and 66 guns in 1844, and 40,523 men and 94 guns in 1845), and plans on using the newly conquered territory of Sindh as a springboard to advance on the Sikh-held region of Multan,[[52]](#cite_note-55) eventually resulted in conflict with the British.

#### Decline

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Two_drawings_of_Sikh_troops_in_action_against_British_forces.jpg/250px-Two_drawings_of_Sikh_troops_in_action_against_British_forces.jpg)](/wiki/File:Two_drawings_of_Sikh_troops_in_action_against_British_forces.jpg)

Two late 19th century drawings of Sikh troops in action against British forces during the Anglo-Sikh Wars

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Samadhi_of_Ranjit_Singh_1.jpg/250px-Samadhi_of_Ranjit_Singh_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Samadhi_of_Ranjit_Singh_1.jpg)

The [Samadhi of Ranjit Singh](/wiki/Samadhi_of_Ranjit_Singh "Samadhi of Ranjit Singh") is located in [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), Pakistan, adjacent to the iconic [Badshahi Mosque](/wiki/Badshahi_Mosque "Badshahi Mosque")

After Ranjit Singh's death in 1839, the empire was severely weakened by internal divisions and political mismanagement. This opportunity was used by the [British East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") to launch the [First Anglo-Sikh War](/wiki/First_Anglo-Sikh_War "First Anglo-Sikh War").

The [Battle of Ferozeshah](/wiki/Battle_of_Ferozeshah "Battle of Ferozeshah") in 1845 marked many turning points, the British encountered the [Khalsa Army](/wiki/Punjab_Army "Punjab Army"), opening with a gun-duel in which the Sikhs "had the better of the British artillery". As the British made advances, Europeans in their army were specially targeted, as the Sikhs believed if the army "became demoralized, the backbone of the enemy's position would be broken".[[53]](#cite_note-56) The fighting continued throughout the night. The British position "grew graver as the night wore on", and "suffered terrible casualties with every single member of the Governor General's staff either killed or wounded".[[54]](#cite_note-Maharaja_Ranjit_Singh_p.136-137-57) Nevertheless, the British army took and held Ferozeshah. British General Sir [James Hope Grant](/wiki/James_Hope_Grant "James Hope Grant") recorded: "Truly the night was one of gloom and forbidding and perhaps never in the annals of warfare has a British Army on such a large scale been nearer to a defeat which would have involved annihilation."[[54]](#cite_note-Maharaja_Ranjit_Singh_p.136-137-57)

The reasons for the withdrawal of the Sikhs from Ferozeshah are contentious. Some believe that it was treachery of the non-Sikh high command of their own army which led to them marching away from a British force in a precarious and battered state. Others believe that a tactical withdrawal was the best policy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Sikh empire was finally dissolved at the end of the [Second Anglo-Sikh War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Sikh_War "Second Anglo-Sikh War") in 1849 into separate [princely states](/wiki/Princely_states "Princely states") and the British [province of Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_(British_India) "Punjab (British India)"). Eventually, a Lieutenant Governorship was formed in Lahore as a direct representative of the [British Crown](/wiki/British_Crown "British Crown").

## Administration and state

### Government

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Detail_from_%E2%80%98Darbar_of_Maharaja_Ranjit_Singh%E2%80%99%2C_gouache%2C_ca.1850.jpg/250px-Detail_from_%E2%80%98Darbar_of_Maharaja_Ranjit_Singh%E2%80%99%2C_gouache%2C_ca.1850.jpg)](/wiki/File:Detail_from_%E2%80%98Darbar_of_Maharaja_Ranjit_Singh%E2%80%99,_gouache,_ca.1850.jpg)

Detail from ‘[Darbar](/wiki/Durbar_(court) "Durbar (court)") (royal court) of Maharaja [Ranjit Singh](/wiki/Ranjit_Singh "Ranjit Singh")’, [gouache](/wiki/Gouache "Gouache"), ca.1850

The empire's ruling court based out of Lahore is termed the *Lahore Darbar* or *Khalsa Darbar*.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22)[[18]](#cite_note-:11-20) Faqir Saifuddin of the [Fakir Khana Museum](/wiki/Fakir_Khana "Fakir Khana") prefers to use the term *Punjab Darbar* rather than "Khalsa Darbar", owing to the large role Muslims played in Ranjit Singh's court.[[55]](#cite_note-58) The ruling court was diverse and under the ultimate command of the ruling *maharaja*, who was the "drum of the Khalsa".[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) In-theory, the Sikh court was based on the Khalsa ideals propounded by Guru Gobind Singh yet the court was secular in-practice.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) As an example of this secularism, members of the court came from various religious background, including Sikhs, Hindus, Muslims, and Christians.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) Furthermore, the members also came from various ethnic, regional, and caste backgrounds, such as Dogras, Rajputs, Brahmins, Jats, and Europeans.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) Whilst Ranjit Singh himself preferred to dress modestly, the Sikh court was filled with elaborately garbed and decorated members.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) Only three individuals were permitted to be seated on chairs within the durbar, them namely being heir-apparent [Kharak Singh](/wiki/Kharak_Singh "Kharak Singh"), *[Kanwar](/wiki/Kunwar "Kunwar")* [Sher Singh](/wiki/Sher_Singh "Sher Singh"), and Raja Hira Singh.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) Three sides of the hall of the durbar were covered with golden-pillars, with shawl carpets that were embroidered with gold and silver, and inset with precious stones, decorating the floor.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) The maharaja was seated on a [golden throne](/wiki/Maharaja_Ranjit_Singh%27s_throne "Maharaja Ranjit Singh's throne"), with Ranjit Singh preferring to sit cross-legged on it.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) The member of the court allowed to be seated behind the maharaja was Raja [Dhian Singh](/wiki/Dhian_Singh "Dhian Singh").[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) The rest of the members of the court were seated as per their rank and status.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) The colours of the Sikh court were yellow and green.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) Thus, most of its members donned yellow-coloured dressings made from Kashmiri silk or woolens.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) However, there existed no strict categorisation scheme of the rankings of the constituent members of the Lahore Darbar, thus the rankings of its members was determined by the level of trust the maharaja held in them.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) The court also granted awards upon its members, with most of these essentially being [bestowed titles](/wiki/Sikh_titles "Sikh titles") in the form of honorifics, however some members were granted *[jagirs](/wiki/Jagir "Jagir")* (estate grant).[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) Laziness was heavily looked-down upon with the court, with the ruling maharaja often sending out the court's members on military or diplomatic missions.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22)

The business of the ruling government was carried out in Lahore, specifically the Musamman Burj located within the [Lahore Fort](/wiki/Lahore_Fort "Lahore Fort").[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) A public court was held from morning until noon in the *Diwan-i-Aam* ("hall of audience"), with the court being attended by important members of the court, including princes, ministers, nobles, and civil and military officers.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) Some matters discussed in the court include high-level civil and military appointments, reports from the [provincial governors](/wiki/Subahdar "Subahdar") (provincial *[satraps](/wiki/Satrap "Satrap")*) and *kardars* (tax collectors).[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) When matters of the court were read-out, royal decrees made orally were transferred into writing for final approval.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) Tributes and *nazaranas* were also exchanged or bestowed within the court.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) Supplicants to the court were dismissed with *khill'ats* (robes) or monetary gifts.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) When the maharaja was travelling, the court ceased to be static and was held at whatever location the maharaja's retinue decided to hold-up at, often under a tree or whilst moving on horseback.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) The maharaja would dictate orders to provincial governors whilst inspecting their troops or even in the midst of battle.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22)

Noble members of the court, including relatives of the royal family, resided in palatial *[haveli](/wiki/Haveli "Haveli")* structures and donned expensive clothing and accessories (such as jewellery).[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) The Sikh princes, and also Raja Dhian Singh, were permitted to hold their own miniature *durbars* (courts).[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22)

### Foreign affairs

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Letter_sent_from_the_King_of_France%2C_Louis-Philippe%2C_to_Maharaja_Ranjit_Singh._Ranjit_Singh_is_addressed_as_%E2%80%9CRendjit_Sing_Bahador_-_Padichah_du_Pendjab%E2%80%9D._Dated_27_October%2C_1835.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Letter_sent_from_the_King_of_France,_Louis-Philippe,_to_Maharaja_Ranjit_Singh._Ranjit_Singh_is_addressed_as_%E2%80%9CRendjit_Sing_Bahador_-_Padichah_du_Pendjab%E2%80%9D._Dated_27_October,_1835.jpg)

A letter sent from the King of France, [Louis-Philippe](/wiki/Louis_Philippe_I "Louis Philippe I") to Maharaja Ranjit Singh. Ranjit Singh is addressed as "Rendjit Sing Bahador – Padichah du Pendjab". 27 October 1835

Foreign visitors to the Sikh court were treated with respect and hospitality, with many contemporary accounts of foreign visitors to the court noting the good-treatment afforded to them by the state.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) When a foreign visitor arrived, they would be greeted by a protocol officer, who would arrange for their temporary residence, which was based upon the status of the visitor.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) The state government paid for the expenses regarding the visitor's entertainment.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) There exists accounts of visitors being gifted by the state, with presents such as fruits, sweets, wines, and also money.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) Full displays of the empire's regalia and military forces were displayed during important ceremonial functions, such as the marriages of important nobles or when receiving high-level foreign diplomatic dignitaries.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22)

In order to keep tabs and updated on the happenings of surrounding regions, including remote parts of its territory and foreign countries, the Sikh court received reports from the *waqa'nawis* (news-writers) located in the empire's provinces (*subas*).[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) The reports were dispatched to the Lahore Darbar at regular intervals.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) Furthermore, the *vakils* (agents) of foreign countries were associated with the Sikh court on a reciprocal basis.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) The Sikh court had news-writers located in Afghanistan and also had its own *vakil* emissaries in the [Cis-Sutlej States](/wiki/Cis-Sutlej_states "Cis-Sutlej states") and also in territory under the British East India Company's rule.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) Other vakil emissaries of the Sikh court were sent to [Rajputana](/wiki/Rajputana "Rajputana"), the [Marathas](/wiki/Maratha_Confederacy "Maratha Confederacy"), and [Nepal](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Nepal "Kingdom of Nepal") on complimentary missions.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22)

Western/European officers from various backgrounds, including Britishers, Frenchmen, Germans, Italians, Spaniards, Americans, and Russians, also rose to high levels within the Sikh court in many instances.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) However, the Sikh court was wary of the Westerners within the court, and kept them therefore under strict regulation.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) These foreign Western members of the court were persuaded by the state to integrate themselves by marrying a local woman, settling down within the empire, and adopt the cultural customs of the locals, such as growing out a beard or wearing a turban.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) The Western members of the court were also banned from publicly [consuming beef](/wiki/Cattle_slaughter_in_India "Cattle slaughter in India") or [smoking](/wiki/Prohibitions_in_Sikhism "Prohibitions in Sikhism").[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22)

The Sikh Empire did enact a simple border policy where it did not allow uninvited foreigners into the state.[[56]](#cite_note-:10-59) The purpose of this border policy was to dissuade potential foreign spies from entering the country.[[56]](#cite_note-:10-59) An example of the policy in-action is the case of Captain Arnold Mathews, who snuck into the Sikh Empire in circa 1808 to spy under the guise of being a tourist headed towards Kashmir but was intercepted and brought to Lahore.[[56]](#cite_note-:10-59)

### Holidays

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Maharaja_Ranjit_Singh_holding_court_outside_near_the_Golden_Temple_in_Amritsar_with_everyone_dressed_in_Basant_%28yellow%29.jpg/250px-Maharaja_Ranjit_Singh_holding_court_outside_near_the_Golden_Temple_in_Amritsar_with_everyone_dressed_in_Basant_%28yellow%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maharaja_Ranjit_Singh_holding_court_outside_near_the_Golden_Temple_in_Amritsar_with_everyone_dressed_in_Basant_(yellow).jpg)

Maharaja Ranjit Singh holding court outside near the Golden Temple in Amritsar with everyone dressed in *Basant* (yellow)

The Sikh court observed the festivals of [Vaisakhi](/wiki/Vaisakhi "Vaisakhi"), [Dussehra](/wiki/Vijayadashami "Vijayadashami"), [Basant](/wiki/Basant_(festival) "Basant (festival)"), [Holi](/wiki/Holi "Holi"), and [Diwali](/wiki/Diwali "Diwali").[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) Vaisakhi was considered an especially auspicious celebration within the Sikh court, with it giving and distributing gifts of money, gold, silver, cows, horses, elephants, gold-bangles, and food to Brahmins and the poor.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) During Basant celebrations, the military troops of the empire were paraded donning yellow uniforms, with members of the Sikh court and nobles also wearing yellow clothing on the day.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) During Basant, the officials bore gifts for the sovereign ruler, with the ruler in-turn bestowing robe-of-honours to the officials based on their rank and status.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22) During celebrations of Dussehra, the Sikh court assembled itself at Amritsar and the *jagirdari* troops of the empire's military were paraded and inspected by the maharaja.[[19]](#cite_note-:9-22)

### Geography

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/3/30/Joppen1907India1805a.jpg/250px-Joppen1907India1805a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joppen1907India1805a.jpg)

The Indian subcontinent in 1805.

The Sikh Empire spanned a total of over 200,000 sq mi (520,000 km2) at its zenith.[[57]](#cite_note-60)[[58]](#cite_note-61)[[59]](#cite_note-62) Another more conservative estimate puts its total surface area during its zenith at 100,436 sq mi (260,124 km sq).[[60]](#cite_note-:0-63) Jean-Marie Lafont states that the empire comprised a territory of 390,000 square kilometres.[[61]](#cite_note-:13-64)[[62]](#cite_note-:14-65)

The following modern-day political divisions made up the historical Sikh Empire:

* [Punjab region](/wiki/Punjab_region "Punjab region"), to [Mithankot](/wiki/Mithankot "Mithankot") in the south
  + [Punjab, Pakistan](/wiki/Punjab,_Pakistan "Punjab, Pakistan"), excluding [Bahawalpur State](/wiki/Bahawalpur_State "Bahawalpur State")
  + [Punjab, India](/wiki/Punjab,_India "Punjab, India"), excluding the [Cis-Sutlej states](/wiki/Cis-Sutlej_states "Cis-Sutlej states")[[63]](#cite_note-66)
  + [Himachal Pradesh](/wiki/Himachal_Pradesh "Himachal Pradesh"), India, only the territories northwest of Sutlej river.
  + [Jammu Division](/wiki/Jammu_Division "Jammu Division"), [Jammu](/wiki/Jammu "Jammu") and [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir"), India and Pakistan (1808–1846)
* [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir"), from 5 July 1819 to 15 March 1846, India/Pakistan[[64]](#cite_note-67)[[65]](#cite_note-68)
  + [Kashmir Valley](/wiki/Kashmir_Valley "Kashmir Valley"), India from 1819 to 1846
  + [Baltistan](/wiki/Baltistan "Baltistan"), from 1840 onwards[[42]](#cite_note-:3-45)[[43]](#cite_note-:4-46)[[44]](#cite_note-:5-47)
  + [Gilgit](/wiki/Gilgit "Gilgit"), [Gilgit–Baltistan](/wiki/Gilgit%E2%80%93Baltistan "Gilgit–Baltistan"), Pakistan, from 1842 to 1846[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
  + [Ladakh](/wiki/Ladakh "Ladakh"), India 1834–1846[[66]](#cite_note-69)[[67]](#cite_note-70)[[41]](#cite_note-:2-44)
* Lower part of [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa"), Pakistan.[[68]](#cite_note-71)
  + [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), Pakistan[[69]](#cite_note-72) (taken in 1818, retaken in 1834)
  + Excluding [Waziristan](/wiki/Waziristan "Waziristan"), Ranjit Singh made no attempt to conquer Waziristan.[[70]](#cite_note-73)[[71]](#cite_note-74)

[Jamrud](/wiki/Jamrud "Jamrud") District ([Khyber Agency](/wiki/Khyber_Agency "Khyber Agency"), Pakistan) was the westernmost limit of the Sikh Empire. The westward expansion was stopped in the [Battle of Jamrud](/wiki/Battle_of_Jamrud "Battle of Jamrud"), in which the Afghans managed to kill the prominent Sikh general [Hari Singh Nalwa](/wiki/Hari_Singh_Nalwa "Hari Singh Nalwa") in an offensive, though the Sikhs successfully held their position at their Jamrud fort. Ranjit Singh sent his General Sirdar Bahadur Gulab Singh Powind thereafter as reinforcement and he crushed the Pashtun rebellion harshly.[[72]](#cite_note-75) In 1838, Ranjit Singh with his troops marched into Kabul to take part in the victory parade along with the British after restoring Shah Shoja to the Afghan throne at Kabul.[[73]](#cite_note-76)

### Religious policy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Maharaja_Ranjit_Singh_seeking_the_sanctuary_of_Guru_Nanak%2C_ca.1830.jpg/250px-Maharaja_Ranjit_Singh_seeking_the_sanctuary_of_Guru_Nanak%2C_ca.1830.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maharaja_Ranjit_Singh_seeking_the_sanctuary_of_Guru_Nanak,_ca.1830.jpg)

Maharaja Ranjit Singh seeking the sanctuary of Guru Nanak, c. 1830

The Sikh Empire allowed men from religions other than their own to rise to commanding positions of authority.[[74]](#cite_note-77)

The Fakir brothers were trusted personal advisors and assistants as well as close friends to Ranjit Singh,[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin1981ix-78) particularly [Fakir Azizuddin](/wiki/Fakir_Azizuddin "Fakir Azizuddin"), who would serve in the positions of foreign minister of the empire and translator for the maharaja, and played important roles in such important events as the negotiations with the British, during which he convinced Ranjit Singh to maintain diplomatic ties with the British and not to go to war with them in 1808, as British troops were moved along the Sutlej in pursuance of the British policy of confining Ranjit Singh to the north of the river, and setting the Sutlej as the dividing boundary between the Sikh and [British](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") empires;[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198127-79) negotiating with [Dost Muhammad Khan](/wiki/Dost_Muhammad_Khan "Dost Muhammad Khan") during his unsuccessful attempt to retake [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"),[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198127-79) and ensuring the succession of the throne during the Maharaja's last days in addition to caretaking after a stroke, as well as occasional military assignments throughout his career.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198128-80) The Fakir brothers were introduced to the Maharaja when their father, Ghulam Muhiuddin, a physician, was summoned by him to treat an eye ailment soon after his capture of Lahore.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198125-81)

The other Fakir brothers were Imamuddin, one of his principal administrative officers, and Nuruddin, who served as home minister and personal physician, were also granted jagirs by the Maharaja.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin1981iv-82)

Every year, while at Amritsar, Ranjit Singh visited shrines of holy people of other faiths, including several Muslim saints, which did not offend even the most religious Sikhs of his administration.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin19813-83)
As relayed by Fakir Nuruddin, orders were issued to treat people of all faith groups, occupations,[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198119-84) and social levels equally and in accordance with the doctrines of their faith, per the [Shastras](/wiki/Shastras "Shastras") and the [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran"), as well as local authorities like judges and *panches* (local elder councils),[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198117-85) as well as banning forcible possession of others' land or of inhabited houses to be demolished.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198118-86) There were special courts for Muslims which ruled in accordance to Muslim law in personal matters,[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198120-87) and common courts preceded over by judicial officers which administered justice under the customary law of the districts and socio-ethnic groups, and were open to all who wanted to be governed by customary religious law, whether Hindu, Sikh, or Muslim.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198120-87)

One of Ranjit Singh's first acts after the 1799 capture of Lahore was to revive the offices of the hereditary [Qazis](/wiki/Qadi "Qadi") and [Muftis](/wiki/Mufti "Mufti") which had been prevalent in Mughal times.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198120-87) Qazi Nizamuddin was appointed to decide marital issues among Muslims, while Muftis Mohammad Shahpuri and Sadulla Chishti were entrusted with powers to draw up title-deeds relating to transfers of immovable property.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198120-87) The old mohalladari[*[definition needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] system was reintroduced with each [mahallah](/wiki/Mahallah "Mahallah"), or neighbourhood subdivision, placed under the charge of one of its members. The office of [Kotwal](/wiki/Kotwal "Kotwal"), or prefect of police, was conferred upon a Muslim, Imam Bakhsh.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198120-87)

Generals were also drawn from a variety of communities, along with prominent Sikh generals like [Hari Singh Nalwa](/wiki/Hari_Singh_Nalwa "Hari Singh Nalwa"), Fateh Singh Dullewalia, Nihal Singh Atariwala, [Chattar Singh Attariwalla](/wiki/Chattar_Singh_Attariwalla "Chattar Singh Attariwalla"), and Fateh Singh Kalianwala; Hindu generals included [Misr Diwan Chand](/wiki/Misr_Diwan_Chand "Misr Diwan Chand") and [Dewan Mokham Chand Nayyar](/wiki/Dewan_Mokham_Chand "Dewan Mokham Chand"), his son, and his grandson; and Muslim generals included [Ilahi Bakhsh](/wiki/Ilahi_Bakhsh "Ilahi Bakhsh") and Mian Ghaus Khan; one general, [Balbhadra Kunwar](/wiki/Balbhadra_Kunwar "Balbhadra Kunwar"), was a Nepalese Gurkha, and European generals included [Jean-Francois Allard](/wiki/Jean-Francois_Allard "Jean-Francois Allard"), [Jean-Baptiste Ventura](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Ventura "Jean-Baptiste Ventura"), and [Paolo Avitabile](/wiki/Paolo_Avitabile "Paolo Avitabile").[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198123-88) other notable generals of the [Sikh Khalsa Army](/wiki/Sikh_Khalsa_Army "Sikh Khalsa Army") were [Veer Singh Dhillon](/wiki/Veer_Singh_Dillon "Veer Singh Dillon"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Sham Singh Attariwala](/wiki/Sham_Singh_Attariwala "Sham Singh Attariwala"), [Mahan Singh Mirpuri](/wiki/Mahan_Singh_Mirpuri "Mahan Singh Mirpuri"), and [Zorawar Singh Kahluria](/wiki/Zorawar_Singh_Kahluria "Zorawar Singh Kahluria"), among others.

The appointment of key posts in public offices was based on merit and loyalty, regardless of the social group or religion of the appointees, both in and around the court, and in higher as well as lower posts. Key posts in the civil and military administration were held by members of communities from all over the empire and beyond, including Sikhs, Muslims, [Khatris](/wiki/Khatri "Khatri"), [Brahmins](/wiki/Brahmins "Brahmins"), [Dogras](/wiki/Dogras "Dogras"), [Rajputs](/wiki/Rajput "Rajput"), [Pashtuns](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns"), Europeans, and [Americans](/wiki/Americans "Americans"), among others,[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198122-89) and worked their way up the hierarchy to attain merit. [Dhian Singh](/wiki/Dhian_Singh_Dogra "Dhian Singh Dogra"), the prime minister, was a Dogra, whose brothers [Gulab Singh](/wiki/Gulab_Singh "Gulab Singh") and Suchet Singh served in the high-ranking administrative and military posts, respectively.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198122-89) Brahmins like finance minister [Raja Dina Nath](/wiki/Raja_Dina_Nath "Raja Dina Nath"), Sahib Dyal, and others also served in financial capacities.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198123-88)

Muslims in prominent positions included the Fakir brothers, Qazi Nizamuddin, and Mufti Muhammad Shah, among others. Among the top-ranking Muslim officers there were two ministers, one governor and several district officers; there were 41 high-ranking Muslim officers in the army, including two generals and several colonels,[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198123-88) and 92 Muslims were senior officers in the police, judiciary, legal department and supply and store departments.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198123-88) In artillery, Muslims represented over 50% of the numbers while the cavalry had some 10% Muslims from among the troopers.[[12]](#cite_note-:12-14)

Thus, the government was run by an elite corps drawn from many communities, giving the empire the character of a secular system of government, even when built on theocratic foundations.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaheeduddin198124-90)

A ban on cow slaughter, which can be related to Hindu sentiments, was universally imposed in the empire.[[88]](#cite_note-91)[[89]](#cite_note-92) Ranjit Singh also donated large amounts of gold for the plating of the [Kashi Vishwanath Temple](/wiki/Kashi_Vishwanath_Temple "Kashi Vishwanath Temple")'s dome.[[90]](#cite_note-93)[[91]](#cite_note-Madhuri_2007-94)

The Sikhs attempted not to offend the prejudices of Muslims, noted Baron von [Hügel](/wiki/H%C3%BCgel "Hügel"), the Austrian botanist and explorer,[[92]](#cite_note-95) yet the Sikhs were described as harsh. In this regard, Masson's explanation is perhaps the most pertinent: "Though compared to the Afghans, the Sikhs were mild and exerted a protecting influence, yet no advantages could compensate to their Mohammedan subjects, the idea of subjection to infidels, and the prohibition to slay [kine](/wiki/Cattle "Cattle"), and to repeat the [azan](/wiki/Adhan "Adhan"), or 'summons to prayer'."[[93]](#cite_note-96)

According to [Chitralekha Zutshi](/wiki/Chitralekha_Zutshi "Chitralekha Zutshi") and [William Roe Polk](/wiki/William_R._Polk "William R. Polk"), Sikh governors adopted policies that alienated the Muslim population such as the ban on cow slaughter and the [azan](/wiki/Adhan "Adhan") (the Islamic call to prayer), the seizure of mosques as property of the state, and imposed ruinous taxes on [Kashmiri Muslims](/wiki/Kashmiri_Muslims "Kashmiri Muslims") causing a famine in 1832. In addition, [begar](/wiki/Begar "Begar") (forced labour) was imposed by the Sikh administration to facilitate the supply of materials to the imperial army, a policy that was augmented by the successive Dogra rulers.[[94]](#cite_note-97)[[95]](#cite_note-98)[[96]](#cite_note-99) These policies led the Kashmiri Muslim population to emigrate en masse to more lenient neighbouring regions, particularly [Ladakh](/wiki/Ladakh "Ladakh").[[97]](#cite_note-100) As a symbolic assertion of power, the Sikhs regularly desecrated Muslim places of worship, including closing of the [Jamia Masjid of Srinagar](/wiki/Jamia_Masjid,_Srinagar "Jamia Masjid, Srinagar") and the conversion of the [Badshahi Mosque](/wiki/Badshahi_Mosque "Badshahi Mosque") in Lahore to an ammunition store and horse stable, but the empire still maintained Persian administrative institutions and court etiquette; the Sikh silver rupees were minted on the Mughal standard with Persian legends.[[98]](#cite_note-101)[[99]](#cite_note-102)

Christian missionaries had been active in the Punjab even prior to the dissolution of the empire in 1849.[[100]](#cite_note-103)

## Demography

Religions in Khalsa Empire (1800s)[[6]](#cite_note-The_Encyclopedia_of_Empire-7)[[101]](#cite_note-Puri_2693–2701-104): 2694

| Islam | |  | 69% | |
| Hinduism | |  | 24% | |
| Sikhism | |  | 6% | |
| others | |  | 1% | |

The population of the Sikh empire during the time of [Ranjit Singh](/wiki/Ranjit_Singh "Ranjit Singh")'s rule was estimated to be around 12 million people.[[6]](#cite_note-The_Encyclopedia_of_Empire-7) There were 8.4 million [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims"), 2.88 million [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") and 722,000 [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikhs "Sikhs").[[101]](#cite_note-Puri_2693–2701-104)

The religious demography of the empire is estimated to have been just over 10%[[102]](#cite_note-Abhinav_Publications-105) to 12%[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrewal1998113-106) Sikh, 80% Muslim,[[102]](#cite_note-Abhinav_Publications-105) and just under 10% Hindu.[[102]](#cite_note-Abhinav_Publications-105) [Surjit Hans](/wiki/Surjit_Hans "Surjit Hans") gave different numbers by retrospectively projecting the 1881 census, putting Muslims at 51%, Hindus at 40% and Sikhs at around 8%, the remaining 1% being Europeans.[[104]](#cite_note-107) The population was 3.5 million in 1831, according to [Amarinder Singh](/wiki/Amarinder_Singh "Amarinder Singh")'s *The Last Sunset: The Rise and Fall of the Lahore Durbar*.[[105]](#cite_note-108) Hans Herrli in *The Coins of the Sikhs* estimated the total population of the empire to be around 5.35 million during 1838.[[60]](#cite_note-:0-63) Meanwhile, Jean-Marie Lafont estimates that the population of the empire consisted of 15 million people.[[61]](#cite_note-:13-64)[[62]](#cite_note-:14-65)

An estimated 90% of the Sikh population at the time, and more than half of the total population, was concentrated in the upper [Bari](/wiki/Doab#Bari_Doabs "Doab"), [Jalandhar](/wiki/Doaba "Doaba"), and upper [Rachna](/wiki/Rachna_Doab "Rachna Doab") *[Doabs](/wiki/Doab "Doab")*, and in the areas of their greatest concentration formed about one third of the population in the 1830s; half of the Sikh population of this core region was in the area covered by the later districts of Lahore and Amritsar.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrewal1998113-106)

In 1839, a major pogrom, called the *[Allahdad](/wiki/Allahdad "Allahdad")*, targeting the [local Jews](/wiki/Mashhadi_Jews "Mashhadi Jews") of [Mashhad](/wiki/Mashhad "Mashhad") in [Qajar Persia](/wiki/Qajar_Persia "Qajar Persia") had occurred.[[106]](#cite_note-:8-109) A group of [Persian Jewish](/wiki/Persian_Jews "Persian Jews") refugees from Mashhad, escaping persecution back home in Qajar Persia, settled in the Sikh Empire around the year 1839.[[106]](#cite_note-:8-109) Most of the Jewish families settled in [Rawalpindi](/wiki/Rawalpindi "Rawalpindi") (specifically in the Babu Mohallah neighbourhood) and [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar").[[107]](#cite_note-110)[[108]](#cite_note-111)[[109]](#cite_note-112)[[110]](#cite_note-113)[[106]](#cite_note-:8-109) Most of these Jews would leave for the [Dominion of India](/wiki/Dominion_of_India "Dominion of India") during the [partition of 1947](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India").[[111]](#cite_note-114)[[106]](#cite_note-:8-109)

## Timeline

* 1699: Formation of the [Khalsa](/wiki/Khalsa "Khalsa") by Guru Gobind Singh.
* 1710–1716: Banda Singh defeats the [Mughals](/wiki/Mughals "Mughals") and declares [Khalsa](/wiki/Khalsa "Khalsa") rule.
* 1716–1738: Mughals take back territorial control for two decades but Sikhs engage in [guerrilla warfare](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare")
* 1733–1735: The Khalsa accepts, only to reject, the confederal status given by the Mughals.
* 1738–1757: [Nader Shah's invasion of India](/wiki/Nader_Shah%27s_invasion_of_India "Nader Shah's invasion of India"); Afghan invasion by [Ahmad Shah Durrani](/wiki/Ahmad_Shah_Durrani "Ahmad Shah Durrani")
* 1761–1767: Recapture of Punjab region by Afghan in [Third Battle of Panipat](/wiki/Third_Battle_of_Panipat "Third Battle of Panipat")
* 1763–1774: Charat Singh Sukerchakia, Misldar of [Sukerchakia](/wiki/Sukerchakia "Sukerchakia") [misl](/wiki/Misl "Misl"), establishes himself in Gujranwala.
* 1764–1783: Baba [Baghel Singh](/wiki/Baghel_Singh "Baghel Singh"), Misldar of [Singh Krora Misl](/wiki/Singh_Krora_Misl "Singh Krora Misl"), imposes taxes on the Mughals.
* 1783: [Sikh capture of Delhi and the Red Fort](/wiki/Battle_of_Delhi_(1783) "Battle of Delhi (1783)") from the Mughals
* 1773: Ahmad Shah Durrani dies and his son [Timur Shah](/wiki/Timur_Shah_Durrani "Timur Shah Durrani") launches several invasions into Punjab.
* 1774–1790: [Maha Singh](/wiki/Maha_Singh "Maha Singh") becomes Misldar of the Sukerchakia misl. [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Bataille_de_Sobraon.jpg/250px-Bataille_de_Sobraon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bataille_de_Sobraon.jpg)

  Contemporary painting of the [Battle of Sobraon](/wiki/Battle_of_Sobraon "Battle of Sobraon") in 1846.
* 1790–1801: [Ranjit Singh](/wiki/Ranjit_Singh "Ranjit Singh") becomes Misldar of the Sukerchakia misl.
* 1799, formation of the [Sikh Khalsa Army](/wiki/Sikh_Khalsa_Army "Sikh Khalsa Army")
* 12 April 1801 (coronation) – 27 June 1839: reign of Maharaja Ranjit Singh.
* March 1809 – August 1809: [Nepal–Sikh War](/wiki/Nepal%E2%80%93Sikh_War "Nepal–Sikh War")
* 20 February 1810: Siege of Multan (1810)
* 1 June 1813: Ranjit Singh is given the Kohinoor Diamond.
* 13 July 1813: [Battle of Attock](/wiki/Battle_of_Attock "Battle of Attock"), the Sikh Empire's first significant victory over the Durrani Empire.
* – 2 March June 1818: [Battle of Multan](/wiki/Siege_of_Multan_(1818) "Siege of Multan (1818)"), the 2nd battle in the [Afghan–Sikh wars](/wiki/Afghan%E2%80%93Sikh_wars "Afghan–Sikh wars").
* 3 July 1819: [Battle of Shopian](/wiki/Battle_of_Shopian "Battle of Shopian") [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Bataille_d%27Aliwal_1.jpg/250px-Bataille_d%27Aliwal_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bataille_d%27Aliwal_1.jpg)

  The charge of the British 16th Lancers at [Aliwal](/wiki/Battle_of_Aliwal "Battle of Aliwal") on 28 January 1846, during the [First Anglo-Sikh War](/wiki/First_Anglo-Sikh_War "First Anglo-Sikh War")
* 14 March 1823: [Battle of Nowshera](/wiki/Battle_of_Nowshera "Battle of Nowshera")
* 30 April 1837: [Battle of Jamrud](/wiki/Battle_of_Jamrud "Battle of Jamrud")
* 27 June 1839 – 5 November 1840: Reign of Maharaja [Kharak Singh](/wiki/Kharak_Singh "Kharak Singh")
* 5 November 1840 – 18 January 1841: [Chand Kaur](/wiki/Chand_Kaur "Chand Kaur") is briefly [Regent](/wiki/Regent "Regent")
* 18 January 1841 – 15 September 1843: Reign of Maharaja Sher Singh
* May 1841 – August 1842: [Sino-Sikh war](/wiki/Sino-Sikh_war "Sino-Sikh war")
* 15 September 1843 – 31 March 1849: Reign of Maharaja [Duleep Singh](/wiki/Duleep_Singh "Duleep Singh")
* 1845–1846: [First Anglo-Sikh War](/wiki/First_Anglo-Sikh_War "First Anglo-Sikh War")
* 1848–1849: [Second Anglo-Sikh War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Sikh_War "Second Anglo-Sikh War")
